# The District

> Visual journalism about what happens in 3,000+ city halls across America. A publication by Hamlet.

## About

The District analyzes transcripts, votes, and public records from more than 3,000 city halls across America to uncover patterns in local democracy. Every story starts with data — NLP analysis of meeting transcripts, voting records, and government documents — then layers in investigative reporting, external sources, and interactive visualizations.

Website: https://district.myhamlet.com
Publisher: Hamlet (https://www.myhamlet.com)

## Published Articles

### Five Years After Marshall — Boulder Area
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/marshall-five-years
- Published: April 2026
- Analysis of four years of post-Marshall-Fire governing-body meetings across Superior, Lafayette, Broomfield, and Erie Granicus archives. The fire destroyed 1,084 homes across Superior, Louisville, and unincorporated Boulder County on Dec 30, 2021; the City of Boulder itself lost zero. Xcel Energy settled the litigation for $640 million in Sept 2025 without admitting fault. What followed in council chambers was a slow, four-year rezoning around the actuarial re-mapping — ADUs, short-term rentals, grassland fuel management, and institutional-investor lot consolidation.
- Data: 551 meetings ingested, 523 transcribed (94% coverage), 10.78M words of testimony. Pass 1 classification on 8 topics + 4 rhetorical dimensions (Sonnet 4.6). Pass 2 quote extraction (Opus 4.6). Featured-quote curation + narrative arc by Gemini 3 Pro.
- Sources: Boulder County Lessons Learned + After-Action reports, Colorado Division of Insurance underinsurance study, Colorado Wildfire Resiliency Code record (HB 22-1115 / SB 23-166), Colorado FAIR Plan (HB 23-1288), Boulder Reporting Lab, Colorado Public Radio, The Colorado Sun, CBS Colorado, CU Boulder Earth Lab (Jennifer Balch), four Granicus meeting archives.

### The Bill Comes Due — Pittsburgh
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/pittsburghs-bill
- Published: March 2026
- Analysis of 108,394 votes across 25 years of Pittsburgh City Council records reveals a legislature that agrees on 97.3% of everything — then fractures on property taxes, inclusionary housing, and zoning reform. Inclusionary housing passed unanimously six times from 2018–2022 before erupting into a persistent 5–4 seesaw in late 2024. The 2026 property tax rate failed 4–5 before a revised version passed the same night.
- Data: 30,996 matters, 108,394 votes, 43 council members, 1,092 split-vote items, 25 years of records (2001–2026).
- Sources: Legistar voting records, PublicSource, WESA, Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, TribLive, U.S. Census Bureau.

### The Boom That Broke — Austin
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/austin-boom
- Published: March 2026
- Analysis of 315,322 construction permits from Austin's open data portal reveals a city whose building boom peaked at 71,105 permits in 2021 and has since contracted 23%. New single-family home permits crashed 55%. Cleaned construction values fell 71% from their 2022 peak. Meanwhile, 84 government bodies generated 9,455 recorded meetings over 36 years.
- Data: 315,322 permits (2021-2026), 66,156 building permits, $143.2B total construction value, 9,455 Swagit meeting videos, 84 government bodies.
- Sources: City of Austin Open Data Portal (Socrata), U.S. Census Bureau ACS, Austin Swagit meeting archives, Austin American-Statesman, Texas Tribune.

### The City That Won't Agree — Ann Arbor
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/ann-arbor-divided
- Published: February 2026
- Analysis of 8.1 million votes across 142 U.S. cities reveals Ann Arbor has the highest council dissent rate in America at 18.19% — 3x the next competitor, 300x Chicago's rate. Fifteen years of voting data and pairwise agreement analysis expose deep factional lines in an all-Democrat city.
- Data: 53,207 Ann Arbor votes, 409 unique council members, 7,100+ agenda items, 17 years of records (2008-2024).
- Sources: Legistar voting records, Michigan Daily, Ann Arbor Observer, WEMU, U.S. Census Bureau, Dave Askins factional analysis.

### Five Futures for Oakland
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/oaklands-future
- Published: February 2026
- An interactive simulation of Oakland's fiscal crisis with five policy paths across 113 census tracts.
- Data: 437,825 population, $360M structural deficit, 134 council meetings analyzed, 1.26M crime records, $94K median income, $884K median home value.
- Sources: City of Oakland budget, Oakland CrimeWatch (Socrata), U.S. Census ACS, Oakland Housing Element, GreatSchools, BLS, HUD, EPA EJScreen, FEMA.

### The Data Center Gold Rush
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/data-center-gold-rush
- Published: January 2026
- NLP analysis of 156 cities' meeting transcripts for data center sentiment from 5,007 mentions across 2,847 meetings.
- Key finding: Average sentiment is 49.9/100 (nearly split). Power consumption (87 mentions) and water usage (78 mentions) are top concerns. 38 cities skeptical, 40 welcoming, 78 neutral.
- Sources: Fox Business, Data Center Watch, EESI, Capitol News Illinois.

### The Abundance Index
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/abundance-index
- Published: January 2026
- 84 cities ranked by openness to development on a YIMBY-to-NIMBY scale.
- Key finding: Average abundance index is 57.7. Temple, TX is most YIMBY (80.0 index, 100% positive ratio). Fairfax, VA is most NIMBY (12.0 index, 0% positive ratio). 68-point spread between most and least welcoming.
- Sources: Temple Daily Telegram, KXXV, KCEN-TV, Census.gov.

### The Temperature Check
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/temperature-check
- Published: January 2026
- 438 cities measured for civic friction on a 0-100 scale using NLP analysis of public comments.
- Key finding: Average friction score is 85.3/100. Southern states average 97%+ contention rates — over 20 points higher than the Pacific Northwest. Hottest: Sparks NV, Tiffin OH, High Point NC (all score 100). Calmest: Grants Pass OR (38.2).
- Sources: KGW, Supreme Court (Grants Pass v. Johnson), Census.gov.

### The Vote Tracker
- URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/vote-tracker
- Published: January 2026
- Analysis of 25,219 votes across 1,524 local officials from multiple cities.
- Key finding: Average approval rate is 96.5%. Newark officials vote yes 100% of the time. Princeton is the most divided, with environmental votes (75% approval) being the most contested category.
- Sources: Gothamist, The Daily Princetonian, Chicago Sun-Times, UIC Political Science.

## Data Sources

The District draws from:
- Legistar meeting transcripts (3,000+ cities)
- Socrata open data portals
- U.S. Census Bureau (ACS 5-year estimates)
- Local government budget documents
- FBI/BLS/EPA/HUD/FEMA federal datasets
- Local news reporting and academic research

### The Most Deliberative Square Mile in America
URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/piedmonts-deliberation
Published: March 2026
Summary: Analysis of 461 government meetings (9.3 million words) from Piedmont, California — a tiny, wealthy city entirely surrounded by Oakland. Covers five governing bodies across six years (2020-2026), examining deliberation volume, speaker patterns, topic distribution, and the flashpoint meetings where national crises met local governance.

### Three Cities, One Fire Zone — Lamorinda
URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/lamorinda-triangle
Published: March 2026
Summary: Analysis of government meetings across Lafayette, Orinda, and Moraga — three wealthy Contra Costa County cities sharing wildfire risk, school districts, and a fire district, yet governing with fundamentally different approaches. Examines how fire shapes every policy debate from housing to traffic to school budgets.

### Eleven Theories of a Highway — Charlotte
URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/charlotte-the-pause
Published: May 2026
Summary: Single-meeting deep-dive on the February 23, 2026 Charlotte City Council session that produced a request to pause the I-77 South Express Lanes — an NCDOT toll-lane expansion priced at $3.2B-to-$4B+ ($600M state share) cutting through the historically Black west-side neighborhoods of Wilmore, McCrorey Heights, Wesley Heights, Reid Park, Revolution Park, and Brookhill. Of Charlotte's eleven council members, eight took the floor on the project; Mayor Vi Lyles presided. LaWana Mayfield was absent; Joi Mayo (D3) and Victoria Watlington (At-Large) did not speak on I-77. Each speaker articulated a different theory of leverage: agenda-rule reform (Johnson), retreat agenda (Graham), trust as the lever (Mazuera Arias, Owens), procurement-timeline scrutiny (Ajmera), nine months of constituent work (Anderson, D1), and route-to-staff (Lyles). Ed Driggs (D7) was the lone Republican and the only member to argue against the pause — and was also Charlotte's CRTPO delegate at the October 16, 2024 vote that backed NCDOT's plan, casting Charlotte's full 46% weighted bloc in a 14-7 motion that WFAE's Steve Harrison has called "the deciding vote." The article holds WFAE's "fragmented but silent" reading in tension with a "deliberate intellectual diversity" reading, names the organized opposition (Sustain Charlotte, Black Political Caucus of Charlotte-Mecklenburg, Action NC), and notes the May 11, 2026 council vote on a follow-on resolution directing CRTPO to withhold support. Charlotte holds 46% of the weighted CRTPO board vote.
Data: Full transcript of meeting b7162e3d (YouTube hDzHEqyj9Qs) via AssemblyAI Best speech model with diarization, attributed via Gemini 3.1 Pro one-shot mapping (mean applied confidence 0.97). I-77 mention frequency baseline derived from 40 Charlotte transcripts (April 2024 – April 2026): 23 mentions in February 2026 vs. <2/month average; April followed at 13. Roster from canonical-v1 entity resolution: 11 council members (4 at-large + 7 district: Anderson D1, Graham D2, Mayo D3, Johnson D4, Mazuera Arias D5, Owens D6, Driggs D7) + Mayor Lyles. Mitchell elected Mayor Pro Tem 12/1/2025, beating Ajmera 8-4.
Sources: City of Charlotte Legistar, CLTgov YouTube channel (Feb 23 2026 meeting), WFAE 90.7 (Steve Harrison's "bait-and-switch" reporting Feb 23, "potent political issue" Apr 6, "council blinked" March 2, May 11 agenda preview Apr 28 2026), WBTV (April 29 2026 resolution preview), WCNC, Axios Charlotte (Nov 6 2025 historic-Black-sites piece), Charlotte Post (Dec 30 2025 racial-bias rally piece), NCDOT I-77 South Express Lanes project records, CRTPO meeting minutes.

### How Local Government Works — The Definitive Guide
URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works
Published: March 2026
Summary: The most comprehensive guide to American local government ever published. 14 chapters covering the full landscape: 90,837 government entities (2022 Census of Governments), $2.32 trillion in annual spending, 500,396 elected officials, 14.2 million public employees. Chapters cover government types and taxonomy, five forms of municipal government (council-manager at 48%, strong mayor at 30%), roles and compensation (mayor salaries from $30K to $353K), lawmaking processes, public meetings and open meeting laws (all 50 states), land use and zoning (77.8% of California residential land zoned single-family), municipal finance (72% of local tax revenue from property taxes, $1.34T unfunded pensions), elections (26% average off-cycle turnout), citizen engagement (95% of public commenters are white per Einstein/Glick/Palmer), legal framework (Dillon's Rule vs home rule, state preemption), county government (3,143 counties), infrastructure ($3.7T investment gap), and future challenges.
Data sources: 2022 Census of Governments (U.S. Census Bureau), ICMA Municipal Form of Government Survey, Tax Policy Center, Urban Institute, Brookings Institution, ASCE 2025 Infrastructure Report Card, Einstein/Glick/Palmer participation research (Boston University), National League of Cities, DOJ Ferguson investigation, Northwestern Medill Local News Initiative.
Chapters:
- I. Why It Matters: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/why-it-matters
- II. The Taxonomy: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/taxonomy
- III. Forms of Government: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/forms
- IV. The Roles: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/roles
- V. Lawmaking: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/lawmaking
- VI. The Meeting: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/the-meeting
- VII. Land Use & Zoning: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/land-use
- VIII. The Money: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/money
- IX. Elections: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/elections
- X. Citizen Power: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/engagement
- XI. Legal Framework: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/legal-framework
- XII. County Government: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/county-government
- XIII. Infrastructure: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/infrastructure
- XIV. The Future: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/challenges

### Glossary of Local Government Terms
URL: https://district.myhamlet.com/articles/how-local-government-works/glossary
Published: March 2026
Summary: 45 key terms in American local government defined for general readers. Covers structure (municipality, county, special district, charter city), process (consent calendar, ordinance, variance, public hearing), law (Dillon's Rule, home rule, preemption, Brown Act, CEQA, eminent domain), finance (property tax, enterprise fund, fiscalization, RHNA), roles (city manager, city clerk, city attorney), and elections (at-large, district, off-cycle, recall, ranked choice voting). Each term links to the chapter(s) where it is discussed.

## Contact

For corrections, data requests, or press inquiries: https://www.myhamlet.com
